name: Marble Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Marble. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Marble surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Marble absorption and minimal subsurface damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on Marble surfaces

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal while preserving Marble substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal effects

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for thorough cleaning with progressive contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage and processing efficiency

power.description: Typical range for Nd:YAG lasers in stone cleaning to balance efficiency and heat control; starting at 100 W avoids thermal cracking in marble (CaCO3, low thermal conductivity ~2.5 W/mK); max limited to prevent decomposition above 800°C. Source: Adapt Laser Systems specs and Verho et al. (2007) in J. Cult. Herit.

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds for heavy contamination (e.g., soot) ensure complete removal without residue; faster for light dirt; 500 mm/s optimal for uniform cleaning on porous marble surfaces. Source: Industry manuals from Laser Photonics and case studies in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2015).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred over ps/fs for cost-effective cleaning with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm) on heat-sensitive marble; avoids plasma shielding issues in fs. Source: Laser physics in Pouli et al. (2010), Appl. Phys. A.

repetition_rate.description: 20 kHz balances cleaning speed and heat accumulation (marble reflectivity ~0.3 at 1064 nm leads to ~70% absorption); higher rates risk cumulative heating/cracking. Source: Academic lit. in J. Appl. Phys. (2012) and IPG Photonics guidelines.

energy_density.description: Fluence below marble damage threshold (~5-8 J/cm² for CaCO3 ablation) for safe contaminant removal; 1 J/cm² effective for organic/inorganic dirt without substrate etching. Source: Ablation studies in Sansonetti & Marchesi (2001), Stud. Conserv., and threshold data from ELZA laser research.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots for fine details on marble sculptures; larger for industrial surface prep; 200 μm recommended for even energy distribution on irregular surfaces. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent Inc. and process opt. in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2018).

pass_count.description: Multi-pass for layered contamination (e.g., 2 passes for heavy industrial grime); single pass sufficient for light cleaning; minimizes total exposure time on thermally fragile marble. Source: Industry case studies in J. Cult. Herit. (2014) and practical guidelines from Laser Cleaning Europe.

overlap_ratio.description: 70% overlap ensures uniform fluence coverage without gaps on marble's textured surface; higher for heavy soiling to avoid streaking. Source: Process optimization in Opt. Express (2016) and scanning system manuals from scanning galvo providers like Scanlab.
